coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of high efficient photoluminescent sunlight driven photocatalyst of N-Carbon Quantum Dots
coredata.dc:creator: Aghamali, Ali
Khosravi, Morteza
Hamishehkar, Hamed
Modirshahla, Nasser
Behnajady, Mohammad A.
coredata.dc:description: A one-step simple, novel and efficient route for the preparation of N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) has been established through the hydrothermal method, using diethylenetriamine (DETA) and citric acid (CA) as the surface passivation agent and carbon source, respectively. The as-prepared N-CQDs exhibit excellent photostability emission property and excitation-independent emission behavior with a high quantum yield up to 95%, which is the maximum value attained until now. As well as the Photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the obtained N-CQDs is pH independent over a range of 4–12 and their PL intensity retains unchanged even after several hours of UV excitation and over six months of storage, presenting an outstanding stability. Most importantly, the experimental results showed that N-CQDs have exceptional photocatalytic performance in the photodegradation of Methyl Blue (MB) under visible and natural sunlight by in situ photogenerated of H2O2 in reaction media. The improved photocatalytic performance of N-CQDs was attributed to the important role of N dopant into CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: High photoluminescence N-CQDs
Photocatalytic performance
In situ photogeneration H2O2

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of photogeneration of hydrogen peroxide in reaction media
Structural and morphological characterization of the N-CQDs
Photocatalytic activity of N-doped carbon quantum dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the financial support of Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, the Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative Council ( INIC ) [6958], Tabriz Branch, Islamic Azad University and Department of Chemistry, North Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation procedure of as-obtained N-CQDs by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid (CA) in the presence of diethylentriamine (DETA).

(a, c) Diluted solution of the as-prepared N-CQDs in aqueous solution is colorless under visible and daylight, (b,d)left, intense blue fluorescence under direct sunlight and right, under 365 nm UV light illumination.
QY measurement of N-CQDs using quinine sulfate as the reference.

(a) FTIR spectrum of as-prepared N-CQDs (b) UV–visible absorption, excitation and emission spectra of N-doped carbon quantum dots in water solution and PL emission of synthesized N-CQDs extended into the near infrared (NIR) wavelength range (Fig. 4(b), inset) (c-e) Fluorescent images taken under the excitation of UV, blue and green light (scale bars: 10 µm).

(a) The PL spectra of the N-CQDs excited at different excitation wavelengths. (b) The corresponding normalized PL emission. (c) The up-converted emission of N-CQDs excited by different excitation wavelengths. (d) Effect of different pH on the PL of N-CQDs.
(a) Photostability of N-CQDs in comparison with fluorescein. (b) Zeta potential of N-CQDs.
(a) TEM image of N-CQDs. (b) SEM image of prepared N-CQDs. (c) DLS. (d) EDX analysis, and inset, related results of element analysis. (e) XRD pattern N-CQDs. (f) Raman spectra of as-prepared N-CQDs.

(a) Absorption spectra of the MB solution at different photocatalytic degradation in presence of N-CQDs and Visible light irradiation, (b) degradation under natural sunlight and (c)Comparison of Photocatalytic degradation rate profile of methylene blue under natural sunlight and visible light irradiation, versus irradiation exposure time.
Schematic diagrams for the possible mechanism for photocatalytic generation of H2O2 by N-CQDs.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of high efficient photoluminescent sunlight driven photocatalyst of N-Carbon Quantum Dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, North Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, North Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Tabriz Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Tabriz Branch, Islamic Azad University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A one-step simple, novel and efficient route for the preparation of N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) has been established through the hydrothermal method, using diethylenetriamine (DETA) and citric acid (CA) as the surface passivation agent and carbon source, respectively. The as-prepared N-CQDs exhibit excellent photostability emission property and excitation-independent emission behavior with a high quantum yield up to 95%, which is the maximum value attained until now. As well as the Photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the obtained N-CQDs is pH independent over a range of 4–12 and their PL intensity retains unchanged even after several hours of UV excitation and over six months of storage, presenting an outstanding stability. Most importantly, the experimental results showed that N-CQDs have exceptional photocatalytic performance in the photodegradation of Methyl Blue (MB) under visible and natural sunlight by in situ photogenerated of H2O2 in reaction media. The improved photocatalytic performance of N-CQDs was attributed to the important role of N dopant into CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), one of the newest nano allotropes of carbon, were accidently found in 2004, as a fraction in electrophoretic separation of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotubes derived from arc discharge soot [1]. CQDs are fluorescent nanometer-scale components that comprise an electron-rich core arranging of carbon in order to simplification bioconjugation. CQDs have gently become an emerging star in the nanocarbon allotropes family duo to their harmless, low-priced nature and plentiful in comparison with organic dyes and conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [2–5]. CQDs have many good aspects, such as high aqueous solubility, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, high resistance to photobleaching, photo-induced electron transfer and tunable light emitting. It is worth mentioning that, CQDs have recently demonstrated exceptional abilities as upconversion nanoparticles being capable to successively absorb two or more longer wavelength (low-energy) excitation photons that are emitted as shorter wavelength (higher-energy), which indicate that CQDs can performance as an efficient component in an extensive range of activities including: energy conversion/storage, fluorescent probes, drug delivery, diagnostics, optoelectronic devices, bioimaging, and photocatalyst [4,6–12]. Between all potential applications CQDs have been predicted that possess extraordinary visible-light sensitive photocatalytic performance. However CQDs are promising components for high-efficiency composite photocatalyst design. Furthermore, carbon quantum dots can execute as both electron donors and acceptor, which is the main factor in improving the photocatalytic property [13,14]. Hence, carbon quantum dots possess good performance in trapping and transferring of electrons same as the role of conventional semiconductor and have displayed photocatalysis activity. Although, many investigations have been published on synthesis and properties of CQDs, nonetheless, only few effort reported on the application of CQDs which can act directly as photocatalyst alone [15,16]. Although the coupling of CQDs with common photocatalyst like TiO2 can expand the photo-response range of photocatalyst, as a result promote photocatalysis performance [17,18]. In additional, it has been reported that CQDs represented separation of photogeneration electrons and holes and visible-light sensitizers which, expedite the photocatalytic reaction. Regarding their remarkable characteristics, there has recently been growing interest in the improvement of CQDs and CQDs/semiconductor nanocomposites to enhance photocatalytic activity for complete destruction of pollutants and the reduction of contamination [4,16,19–22]. According to the above-mentioned properties of CQDs, it seems that synthesis method and type of precursor can play a main role in the performance of this type of material. Currently, scientists have made great attempts to synthesize CQDs with exceptional properties and capabilities. Hence, nowadays, researchers fundamentally have focused on the synthesis of CQDs. Since CQDs were first presented in the literature, various synthesis approaches have been developed. In general, the established techniques can be divided into two major strategies: bottom-up and top-down directions. Top-down procedures consist of the disintegration of bulk carbon materials into nanosized graphical subdivisions using chemical or physical processes which consist of electrochemical oxidation [23], laser ablation [24,25], arc discharge [1], strong acid oxidation [26]. Though there is almost no detectable fluorescence in the as-prepared nanosized graphite particles, passivation step is required to attain PL emission light. However, bottom-up methods are procedures that produce CQDs in molecular carbon material such as citric acid and glucose while changing them into small-sized carbon particles through chemical reactions e.g. microwave [7,27], pyrolysis [28], combustion [29], ultrasonic [16,30] and hydrothermal methods [31–33].

Though, these approaches are typically associated with multipart methods and difficult synthetic setting. Above all, due to troublous purification and separation or accumulation during the process, low quantum yields (QY) of CQDs are unavoidable in the all above-mentioned methods [4,23,34–36]. Hence, an efficient method to synthesize photoluminescent CQDs with high quantum yield and purity seems to be really necessary. In conclusion it seems that mainly problems relating to synthesis methods are shortcoming in choice of appropriate carbon sources, kind of synthesis approaches and the effective control of carbon quantum dot size and increase of quantum yields. Among many kinds of this method, the most widely used is the hydrothermal method. Hydrothermal production method based on aqueous solutions method is considered to be unique of the easiest and best gainful ways duo to its easy operation, inexpensive device, low energy and time consumption and appropriate preparation can be attained in a one-pot without multistage operation [33,37]. Given that, the photo-physical and structural features of the CQDs obtained by hydrothermal method are prominently decided by the initial precursor materials [38]. For that reason, the smart choice of initial material for the hydrothermal fabrication of CQDs is of excessive significance in the discovery of new CQDs with exceptional properties.

Whereas, the extensive light absorption properties of carbon quantum dots has motivated their use in photocatalysis, but the transfer of electrons inside the structure of CQDs is insignificant, which has hindered their utilization in the field of photocatalyst. In order to enhance their photocatalytic performance a proficient approach is to adjust electrical constructions doping of nitrogen and amine into carbon quantum dots. Recently, doping the CQDs with nitrogen atoms has been confirmed to be a feasible approach for improving the quantum yield (QY) of CQDs because the nitrogen-doping process can tune the intrinsic electronic and surface properties of CQDs [39–42]. It is also confirmed that surface functionalization and N-doping with nitrogenous and amine material not only improve the photoluminescence excitation-emission intensity noticeably but also N-CQDs were confirmed to possess high visible light induced catalytic activity in the photodegradation of organic compounds [16]. Beside, N-doping facilitate the dehydration, carbonization, and the crystal growth of N-CQDs. As well as, has been confirmed that nitrogen doped CQDs in comparison with the undoped CQDs, involving their considerable application potential in synthesis procedure and environmental protection [12,43,44]. Despite all the outstanding properties related with the photocatalytic properties of carbon quantum dots in field of photocatalysis, very few reports related to the application of these materials have been published in scientific literature [45].

Hence, in this study, we reported an easy one-pot, scalable, green, and effective synthetic method to the preparation of monodispersed N-CQDs with highly optical properties and photostability via hydrothermal carbonization citric acid as carbon source and diethylenetriamine as a nitrogenous surface passivation reagent. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report concerning the direct synthesis of this type of N-CQDs without complicated and post treatment procedures. We reported high purity and high size homogeneity N-CQDs with highest quantum yield without any wearisome separation method, which is the maximum worth yet obtained. Unbelievably the as prepared N-CQDs demonstrated diverse emission colors containing red, green and blue when they were excited by different excitation wavelengths, as inspected under a fluorescent microscope. Also for the first time, we confirm the as-prepared N-CQDs are applicable without any oxidizing agent e.g·H2O2 to photocatalytic destruction of MB, and they presented superior photocatalytic activities under visible and natural sunlight.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA·H2O), diethylenetriamine (DETA), methylene blue (MB), sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid were obtained from Merck. Quinine Sulfate Dihydrate (QS·2H2O) 98%, appropriate for reference fluorescence, was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Merckquant hydrogen peroxide kits were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All the materials are analytical reagent grades and used without further purification. A Millipore ultrapure water system was used to provide water with a resistivity of 18.3 MΩcm−1at 25 °C for all experiments.

The N-doped CQDs were synthesized through a facile hydrothermal procedure. In a typical one-step synthesis, 0.44 g citric acid was dissolved in 20 mL of pure water, followed by adding 225 μL diethylenetriamine under agitated, molar ratio of citric acid to diethylenetriamine was 1:4. Then, the colorless transparent solution was transferred into a 30 mL PTPE-lined stainless steel autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 5 h in an electric heating constant temperature Drying Oven. The obtained product was left to cool down naturally, and a transparent light yellow N-CQDs liquid was achieved.

The morphology and size of N-CQDs were investigated by means of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The sample for TEM was prepared through the deposition of 10 μL N-CQDs aqueous solutions onto ultrathin amorphous carbon film-supported copper grids, whereas the water was eliminated through vaporization in air at ambient temperature. The properties of functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs and the crystal structure were explored by Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) on a pressed KBr tablet, and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), respectively. Fluorescence spectra were measured with a JASCO FP-750 fluorescence spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectrum of the samples were determined by an absorption spectrophotometer (UV-2000, Shimadzu Corp.). Raman were obtained by a Handheld Raman Analyzer (Model FirstGuard). The sample for Raman measurements was excited at 1064 nm. DLS and Zeta potential of the N-CQD solution were evaluated at room temperature through Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS). The fluorescence microscopy images were acquired with an Olympus BX51 microscope.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is defined as the ratio of number of emitted fluorescence photons to number of absorbed excitation photons. The QY of the as-prepared nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots was confirmed by comparing their integrated PL intensities (excited at 350 nm). Quinine sulfate with refractive index (η = 1.33) in 0.1 M H2SO4 (quantum yield 0.54 under 350 nm excitation) was selected as a reference [34,35]. The N-CQDs were dissolved in pure water with refractive index (η = 1.33) at various concentrations. Each compound was tested at five different concentrations and all of them had absorbance values less than 0.1 when measured at 350 nm. All the absorbent values of the solutions at the excitation wavelength at 350 nm were recorded through UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2000, Shimadzu Corp) and photoluminescence (PL) emission spectrum of sample solutions were measured by fluorescence spectrometer (JASCO FP-750) at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, that each of emission and excitation slit widths of 5 nm. The integrated PL intensity in the wavelength range from 375 to 620 nm is the area under the PL peak. Then the quantum yield (QY) was determined through the following equation:
QY
CQDs
= 0 .54
(
m
CQDs
/ m
QS
)
(
η
CQDs
2
/
η
QS
2
)
QY is the fluorescence quantum yield, m is the slope from the scheme of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance, and η is the refractive index of the solutions related to CQDs and QS.

The photocatalytic activity of the samples were calculated via the degradation of a model contaminant methylene blue (MB) under visible and natural sunlight. Natural sunlight and visible light a 500 W halogen lamp with a cut-off filter to exclude radiation below 450 nm to make sure that only visible light were used for the irradiation of the dispersion as the light sources. The sunlight intensity (54 ×103 ± 5000 lx) was measured by digital lux-meter during July to August 2016 (11 a.m. to 4 p.m.). The experiments were performed as follows: 10 mL N-doped carbon quantum dots (20 mg L−1) was added in 150 mL MB solution (20 mg L−1). Prior to any irradiation, the suspension containing N-CQDs and dye was magnetically stirred for 1 h in darkness to establish the adsorption/desorption equilibrium between the MB and the N-doped carbon quantum dots. In order to investigate the dye adsorption on N-CQDs, the reaction admixture of MB solution and N-CQDs was agitating in darkness with continuous stirring, before irradiation with visible light or sunlight. In darkness, most sorption of dye was found to be 22% (within 1 h) on the N-CQDs surface and then it was kept constant until the 2 h. As a result, to establish the adsorption-desorption equilibrium, the reaction admixture (dye and N-CQDs) was stirred 60 min in dark condition prior to any procedure. Finally the suspension was agitated and exposed to light illumination. MB was determined spectrophotometrically at λmax= 665 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of photogeneration of hydrogen peroxide in reaction media
ce:section.ce:para: Peroxide test strips (Merckoquant.10011.0001; Merck, Darmstadt, Germany) were also used to test the hydrogen peroxide concentration. The hydrogen peroxide concentration was identified using merckoquant test strips with a detection rang of 0–25 mg L−1. As per the manufacturer's instructions, measurements were recorded after immersion of strip into a solution for 2 s followed by a 5 s exposure to air.

A dilute solution of N-CQDs (0.1 mg/mL) in distillate water was prepared by means of ultrasonication.100 μL N-CQDs solution was diluted to10 mL by using different solutions with pH 1–12, successively. The pH values of these solutions were adjusted by HCl (10 mM) and NaOH (10 mM) solution and last recorded by digital pH-meter. Next the photoluminescence spectra of these solutions were recorded by means of a fluorescence spectrometer.

The scheme of preparation highly PL of N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) is shown in Fig. 1. As clearly shown in Fig. 2(a-d), the diluted solution of the as-synthesized N-CQDs in aqueous solution is colorless under visible and daylight, while it emits intense blue fluorescence under direct sunlight and 365 nm UV light illumination. As can be seen in Fig. 3, the synthesized N-CQDs present an surprisingly large quantum yield of up to 95% using quinine sulfate as a reference, without any tedious post-treatments, which is the highest value yet obtained for photoluminescent carbon quantum dots [46,47].

With this method, the creation and the surface passivation of N-CQDs are performed concurrently. We think that the formation of CQDs and their surface passivation may be accomplished at the same time in hydrothermal procedure of CA in the presence of DETA. In this study the nature of (DETA) precursors for passivation in the hydrothermal fabrication of N-CQDs has a great efficacy and led to the exploration of new CQDs with exceptional properties. The plentiful functional groups, such as amines and carboxyl acids confirmed through (FT-IR) spectra, can present different defects on the surface of N-CQDs, which performing as excitation energy traps and responsible to the various optical properties. As can be seen in Fig. 4(a), it displays two significant bands at 3336–2925 cm−1 and 1716 cm−1 which are related to the stretching vibrations of O-H, N-H and C=O, respectively. The stretching vibration bands of C=C at 1421 cm−1 was submitted the establishment of an unsaturated aromatic rings with graphical structure in the hydrothermal treatment process. Moreover, the peaks at 1222 cm−1 and 1367 cm−1 are representative of the C–O and amide C–N stretch, respectively. Also the peaks at in ca. 1647 and 1550 cm−1 are related to the typical stretching of the C=N and N-H peaks, respectively [48]. All of the above FT-IR results show that a portion of the carbonyl groups belongs to citric acid has been changed into amide groups during the hydrothermal reaction method.

Furthermore, to investigate the optical properties of the N-CQDs, UV–visible absorption, excitation and emission spectra of N-doped carbon quantum dots in water solution are presented in Fig. 4(b), which indicate a main absorption band located at ca. 354 nm, regarding to the amine/carbonyl functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs, which attributed to the n-π * transition and a weak absorption peak situated at 241 nm, which qualified to the π-π * transition of carbon core [33,49]. The peak at 354 nm is typical of N-CQDs, which upon excitation with light caused in a strong blue emission at 431 nm. As demonstrated, an optical absorption band at 354 nm perfectly accommodate with the peak fluorescence excitation. Furthermore, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the PL peak is valued to be 76 nm, the comparatively smaller FWHM corresponds to a narrower N-CQDs size distribution which was in accordance with to the TEM, SEM and DLS analysis.

The optimum excitation and emission wavelengths in the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of N-CQDs were located at 354 nm and 431 nm. A closer look shows that further a visible emission band situated at 431 nm, extraordinarily the PL emission of synthesized N-CQDs could be expanded into the near IR wavelength area (Fig. 4(b), inset). In order to more confirming these observed optical properties of the as-synthesized N-CQDs, the detailed photoluminescence spectra were evaluated by confocal microscope and shown in the Fig. 4(c-e), we can see that under different excitation wavelengths the N-CQDs demonstrated various emission colors predominantly consisting of blue, green and red. The carboxyl and amide containing functional groups on the surface of N-CQD might be responsible for the extension of fluorescence emission, which may be because of the optical selection of various surface defect situations near the Fermi level of N-CQDs [50]. In the related literatures, it is reported that surface groups such as carboxyl, carbonyl and amino groups can introduce more defects on the surface of CQDs with numerous new energy levels between the HOMO (n) and LUMO (π * ) gaps. Hence, in certain excitation energy, effective electron transitions can arise from the new HOMO energy levels to the LUMO. Finally, the excited electrons can be deactivated by radiative recombination, leading to multicolor fluorescence in the blue to the red regions. Therefore, these new levels of generated HOMO energy make it possible to get various emission colors in different excited wavelengths [51].

To more characterize the optical properties of the as-produced N-CQDs, a detailed PL analysis was accomplished through altered excitation wavelengths. It can be seen in Fig. 5(a-b), amazingly, thru the excitation wavelength altering from 300 nm to 400 nm, the emission wavelength shows closely no shift, which is different from that of most N-CQDs [52,53]. Such excitation-independent PL performance could be clarified through extraordinary uniformity of the as-obtained N-CQDs [12]. Besides the strong PL feature, the as-prepared N-CQDs show clear upconversion PL (UCPL) properties. As presented in Fig. 5(c), while the excitation wavelength changes from 600 to 790 nm, the UCPL spectra show an unchanged emission band at ca. 431 nm. Excitingly, the intensity of the peaks increases first and then decreases with increasing excitation wavelength. The intensity of the peaks at the 680–760 nm regions is higher than that at other regions. This UCPL should be related to the multiphoton active manner [50]. These results indicate that absorption N-CQDs is relatively strong, especially in the UV to blue/red spectral region.

The PL properties of synthesized N-CQDs in aqueous solutions are only partially dependent upon the pH scale of the solutions in the highly acidic/basic media. In Fig. 5(d) the variant of PL intensity for each pH (0−12) is shown. It can be observed that a strong PL intensity was considered in the pH range (4−12). The resulting N-CQDs demonstrated a stable and pH independent PL manners; no obvious variation in the PL emission location or the PL intensity was looked through pH 4–12. This is due to the electronic transitions of π–π* and n-π* that are changed by refilling or depleting their valance bands with the variation in pH [54]. Unpredictably, great stability to photobleaching of N-CQDs is detected in pH range of values from 4 to 12, which suggests that the N-CQDs give an exceptional photo-stability in acid–base concentration media. This performance can be related to exceedingly resistant structural morphology of N-CQDs [55]. Also, no clear photobleaching effect was detected by a constant exposure under UV excitation for 6 h, in comparison with fluorescein, as presented in Fig. 6(a), with the enhance in experiment time, the PL intensity of fluorescein decayed to ca. 50% of the initial strength after 5 h. whereas, the as synthesized N-CQDs exhibited excellent photostability during irradiation which, indicating excellent photostability of these N-CQDs. This durable and established PL of N-CQDs could be related to the carboxyl and amide functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs, which create the N-CQDs have a firm surface chemistry in harsh environments [56]. Also, as can be seen in Fig. 6(b), zeta potential was recorded to be − 16.1 eV in pH at 3.2. Therefore, the N-CQDs aqueous solution is very stable, and did not precipitate or aggregation even after being maintained for more than 6 months.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterization of the N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The structural and morphological characterization of N-CQDs were determined using a series of instruments. Fig. 7(a-b) demonstrations Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) of N-CQDs. Both analysis represented that distributed spherical shaped N-CQDs with an average size of 4.5 nm. To further confirm the size of prepared N-CQDs, Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) study was also done. Fig. 7(c), shows a typical DLS histogram of the sizes of the carbon dots obtained. The average size was determined to be 3 nm. This observation further indicates that the N-CQDs agglomerate easily in solid form but remain isolated in water. Also to verify the chemical composition of the synthesized N-CQDs, Elemental analysis was obtained by Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDXS) analysis and presented in Fig. 7(d), and related results of element analysis were shown inset of related figures. Also, there is no unexpected elements being detected, indicating the purity of the synthesized N-doped carbon quantum dots. The crystallinity and structure of the N-doped CQDs was determined by X-ray diffraction techniques. As shown in Fig. 7(e), the XRD pattern of the N-CQDs gives a prominent band centered at 2θ = 25.4, attributed to the diffraction of graphite [002] crystal planes and presenting a d spacing of 0.35 nm. This value is a little bit greater than the 0.335 nm spacing for bulk graphite. It was also demonstrated that having highly disordered carbon with a predominantly amorphous structure. Raman spectrum of the N-CQDs, is shown in Fig. 7(f). Raman spectroscopy is a valuable characterization method for inspecting carbon materials [57]. Two prominent bands were observed at 1416 and 1646 cm−1, which can be assigned to the D and G bands of the disordered and graphitized carbons, respectively. The band located at ca. 1500 cm−1 is related to aromatic carbon, which was also confirmed by the corresponding peak assignments in the FT-IR [58]. The relative intensity of the disordered D band and crystalline G band (ID/IG) is about 1.13. The relative intensity of ID/IG submits that N-CQD have rich surface defects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of N-doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the photocatalytic performance of N-CQDs, we evaluated the photocatalytic ability of N-CQDs for the photo-degradation of Methylene Blue (MB) under visible and natural sunlight. The results in Fig. 8(a-b) demonstrations the variations in the absorption spectra of MB aqueous solution exposed to visible and natural sunlight over time. It can be observed that when the solution containing N-CQDs and MB was exposed by sunlight and visible light, degradation of MB was nearly complete (ca. 97%), hence, it can be said that, the photodegradation process is very efficient. Predominantly, the best catalytic performance of the N-CQDs was attained when this reaction media was exposed to natural sunlight. These results indicate that N-CQDs can be considered as a competent visible and sunlight driven photocatalyst. The doping with N atoms greatly improve the optical properties of CQDs, by way of introducing supplementary new energy levels, effectually generating new electron transfer ways in the band structures of the N-CQDs. Whereas, CQDs are both exceptional electron donors and acceptors the optical excitation in CQDs promote the excited state reduction/oxidation procedures, which are not only responsible for the formation of electron-hole (e-–h+) pairs used for photocatalytic reaction but also caused to observed PL emissions [7,15]. The improved photocatalytic performance of N-CQDs was ascribed to the important role of N dopant into CQDs. It is confirmed that, the N-doping tune the work function of carbon and could efficiently induce charge delocalization [59]. So, the electron transfer ability of carbon materials can be efficiently promoted. Based on above results and literature [15,45,60], a likely mechanism for the key role of N anion doping in the N-CQDs is described for the photocatalytic degradation mechanism. Existence of nitrogen and oxygen atoms in the graphitic structure of N-CQDs could provide a new electric field or upward band bending near the surface of CQDs. The higher upward band bending electric field will separate the e-/h+ pair, reducing the rate of recombination and promoting h+ transport to the surface. When the absorption of a photon can excite an electron from HOMO to the LUMO of N-CQDs, the reductive LUMO electrons (e-) and oxidative HOMO band holes (h+) are created. Hence, the photo-generated electron react with O2 to generate ̇ superoxide radicals and photo-generated hole react with water to produce hydroxyl radical. Lastly, each one of produced radicals with very strong oxidation capability work to accomplish oxidation reduction (redox) reactions with MB which adsorbed on N-CQDs surface. Although a few research groups have studied the photocatalytic activity of sole N-CQDs in degradation of organic compounds. It is worth noting that, in all reported cases, hydrogen peroxide and carbon quantum dots concurrently, have been added to the reaction solution as an important factor for degradation of chemical contaminants [16,23]. Thus, there is no report on the formation of in situ H2O2 in this photocatalytic reaction. To confirm the formation of H2O2 in reaction media, was determined by the colorimetric method, using Merckquant hydrogen peroxide kits, as a commercial available method [61]. The results indicate that concentration of hydrogen peroxide in solution media when exposed to sunlight compared to visible light was ca. twofold, 10 and 5 (mg L−1), respectively. On the other hand, the upconversion characteristic of N-doped carbon quantum dots, causing increased photocatalytic efficiency in sunlight spectrum. We think that the more outstanding capacitance of N-CQDs, especially in sunlight may be due to their prominent excellent light harvesting ability and efficient utilization of the full spectrum of sunlight. According to the results shown in Fig. 5(c), N-CQDs have UCPL property and can convert visible light with longer wavelength (600–790 nm) into photoluminescence with a shorter wavelength (431 nm). As presented in Fig. 8(c), MB could be completely photodegraded during 260 min and 160 min in the presence of N-CQDs when exposed to visible and natural sunlight, respectively. These results suggest that N-CQDs can allow for more efficient utilization of the sunlight to generate more photo-induced electrons and holes, and this is beneficial to the enhancement of photocatalytic activity. According to the experimental results, in the same time period (160 min) in the presence of sunlight, the removal rate is 23% more than when the sample is exposed to visible light. This improved sunlight photo-activity can be ascribed to the UCPL properties of N-CQDs [45].

Hence, based on the above results, we suggested the following mechanism N-CQDs and generation of H2O2 from water and oxygen in reaction media. Hence, schematic model for the photogeneration of H2O2 is shown in Fig. 9. We believe that doping of nitrogenous material in the structure of carbon quantum dots have an excessive positive effect on the photocatalytic performance and the local electrical arrangement of CQDs are improved, which will enhance the formation of broad electric field. The broad electric field, not only cause to be increase the spatial separation of photogenerated electron-hole and prevents their recombination rate, but also cause to promote the redox reaction on surface of N-CQDs. The role of embedded nitrogenous material and carbonyl functional groups (e.g. carbocyclic acid and amide groups) on the surface of CQDs is comparable with acceptor molecules and noble-metal on the semiconductor surface [62]. On the other hand, the interaction between the CQDs with negative surface charge and MB as positive charge molecule which tendency to absorb on the surface of N-CQDs due to electrostatic interactions and in conclusion it can be influenced the photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs.

This study demonstrated an easy and one pot method for the synthesis of high quality luminescent N-CQDs using hydrothermal treatment by using diethylenetriamine (DETA) and citric acid (CA) as surface passivation reagent and carbon source, respectively. The N-CQDs exhibit strong blue emission under natural sunlight and UV light excitation. More prominently, the N-CQDs display an extraordinary QY and excitation independent PL emission. The fluorescent QY of the N-CQDs is up to 95%, which is the maximum worth yet obtained. Also as synthesized N-CQDs show pH-independent PL manners. Most importantly, the as-obtained N-CQDs show high photocatalytic performance for the photo-degradation of methylene blue in visible and natural sunlight by photogenerated of H2O2 in reaction media. The results indicate that H2O2 is undeniably produced during the photocatalytic reaction and causes to the degradation of methylene blue. The results also indicate that the reaction rate is much higher when the dispersions are exposed to sunlight irradiation compared with visible light. We think that intelligently selection of precursors and kind of synthesis approaches of CQDs is the great importance in the exploration of new CQDs with exceptional properties. It should be noted that, this kind of N-CQDs would aid as a favorable alternative for an innovative sort of fluorescence indicator, optical imaging and so on. In conclusion, we expect that this study will attract more attention to N-CQDs as new semiconductor catalyst for application in photocatalytic water purification.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the financial support of Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, the Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative Council (INIC) [6958], Tabriz Branch, Islamic Azad University and Department of Chemistry, North Tehran Branch, Islamic Azad University.
